coredata.dc:title: Ce4+-triggered cascade reaction for ratiometric fluorescence detection of alendronate
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Xia, Meng
Shi, Fengjin
Xia, Yinghui
Sun, Jing
Zhao, Xian-En
Zhu, Shuyun
coredata.dc:description: A ratiometric fluorescence assay for alendronate (ALDS) has been designed with Ce4+-triggered cascade chromogenic reaction. This strategy involves three processes: (1) Ce4+ oxidizes ascorbic acid (AA) into dehydroascorbic acid (DHAA), which then condenses with o-phenlenediamine (OPD) to generate fluorescent 3-(dihydroxyethyl)furo[3,4-b] quinoxaline-1-one (DFQ), presenting the maximum emission at 434 nm; (2) As oxidase-mimics, Ce4+ can oxidize OPD into fluorescent 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) which shows a strong emission at 568 nm; (3) ALDS inhibits the oxidation ability of Ce4+ towards OPD, thus inhibiting the generation of DAP. Accordingly, a homogeneous ratiometric fluorescence system with dual emission comes into being and the presence of ALDS can change the fluorescence intensity ratio obviously. With F 434/F 568 as readout, ALDS can be detected sensitively with the detection limit of 30 nM. Moreover, this ratiometric method was used to analyze ALDS in both human serum and pharmaceutical samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence sensor
Cascade reaction
Oxidase-mimics
Ce4+
O-Phenlenediamine
Ascorbic acid

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The ESI-MS spectra of DFQ (A) and DAP (B).

Fluorescence emission spectra (A) and absorption spectra (B) of OPD (a), Ce4+ + OPD (b), Ce4+ + OPD + AA (c), and Ce4+ + ALDS + OPD + AA (d). [Ce4+] = 80 μM; [OPD] = 1.8 mM; [AA] = 5 μM; [ALDS] = 60 μM.

The influences of reaction time of Ce4+, OPD and AA (A), pH (B), concentration of Ce4+ (C), concentration of OPD (D), concentration of AA (E) and the reaction time between Ce4+ and ALDS (F) on the fluorescence intensities at 434 nm and 568 nm, respectively.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of Ce4+-AA-OPD system with different concentrations of ALDS (0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, and 70 μM). (B) Linear relationship between the F 434/F 568 versus ALDS concentration.

The F 434/F 568 of Ce4+-AA -OPD system in the presence of ALDS and interfering species. (A) The relevant substances are (a) blank, (b) Ser (10 μM), (c) Ala (10 μM), (d) Gly (10 μM), (e) Lys (10 μM), (f) His (10 μM), (g) Glu (10 μM), (h) Arg (10 μM), (i) Asp (10 μM), (j) Val (10 μM), (k) Met (10 μM), (l) Pro (10 μM), (m) DA (1 μM), (n) AA (1 μM), (o) Cys (1 μM), (p) Hcys (1 μM), (q) GSH (1 μM), (r) UA (1 μM) and (s) ALDS (10 μM). (B) The relevant substances are (a) blank, (b) Fe3+ (1 μM), (c) Cu2+ (1 μM), (d) Cr3+ (1 μM), (e) K+ (100 μM), (f) Na+ (100 μM), (g) Ca2+ (100 μM), (h) Mg2+ (100 μM), (i) CO3 2− (100 μM), (j) SO4 2− (100 μM), (k) Cl− (100 μM), (l) urea (10 μM), (m) starch (10 μM), (n) glucose (10 μM), (o) lactose (10 μM), (p) dextrin (10 μM), (q) magnesium stearate (10 μM), and (r) ALDS (10 μM).
The schematic illustration of Ce4+-AA-OPD system for ratiometric fluorescence detection of ALDS.
Comparison of different optical methods for the detection of ALDS.
Analytical results for ALDS in tablets.

head.ce:title: Ce4+-triggered cascade reaction for ratiometric fluorescence detection of alendronate

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qufu Normal University, Qufu 273165, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qufu Normal University, Qufu 273165, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Qinghai Key Laboratory of Qinghai-Tibet Plateau Biological Resources, Northwest Institute of Plateau Biology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Xining 810001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Qinghai Key Laboratory of Qinghai-Tibet Plateau Biological Resources

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Qinghai Key Laboratory of Qinghai-Tibet Plateau Biological Resources, Northwest Institute of Plateau Biology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Xining 810001, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A ratiometric fluorescence assay for alendronate has been developed with Ce4+-triggered cascade chromogenic reaction in combination of the oxidase-like activity of Ce4+ for the first time.

A ratiometric fluorescence assay for alendronate (ALDS) has been designed with Ce4+-triggered cascade chromogenic reaction. This strategy involves three processes: (1) Ce4+ oxidizes ascorbic acid (AA) into dehydroascorbic acid (DHAA), which then condenses with o-phenlenediamine (OPD) to generate fluorescent 3-(dihydroxyethyl)furo[3,4-b] quinoxaline-1-one (DFQ), presenting the maximum emission at 434 nm; (2) As oxidase-mimics, Ce4+ can oxidize OPD into fluorescent 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) which shows a strong emission at 568 nm; (3) ALDS inhibits the oxidation ability of Ce4+ towards OPD, thus inhibiting the generation of DAP. Accordingly, a homogeneous ratiometric fluorescence system with dual emission comes into being and the presence of ALDS can change the fluorescence intensity ratio obviously. With F 434/F 568 as readout, ALDS can be detected sensitively with the detection limit of 30 nM. Moreover, this ratiometric method was used to analyze ALDS in both human serum and pharmaceutical samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Ratiometric fluorescence technique offers an alternative for fluorescence sensors through recording the intensity ratio at two different wavelengths and has sparked widespread interest in analysis of complex samples [1–3]. In contrast to single signal detection, ratiometric fluorescence strategy avoids analyte-independent factors, such as light scattering from the sample matrix, instrumental parameters, microenvironments around and local concentrations of probe [4–7]. Therefore, ratiometric fluorescence technique possesses self-calibrating merits, thus improves the analysis sensitivity and accuracy. Up to now, various fluorescent nanomaterials have been designed and synthesized. For example, metallic nanoclusters, semiconductor quantum dots, metal-organic frameworks, carbon-based nanomaterials and so on find growing interest in the design of ratiometric fluorescence sensors [8–14].

Generally speaking, the construction of a vast majority of nanoprobe-based ratiometric systems were mainly divided into two classes. One usually requires two pre-conjugated nanoprobes. Two of the above-mentioned nanoprobes can assemble into ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobes with dual-emission. Many analytes including metal ions [9], H2O2 [10], small molecules [11], drugs [12], and enzymes [13] have been detected sensitively with these ratiometric strategies. The other is focused on designing and synthesis of single fluorophore with dual-emission or multi-emission [15–19]. For example, dual-emissive BSA-protected gold nanoclusters, silver nanoclusters and carbon dots have been synthesized and applied in designing ratiometric sensors. Nevertheless, the preparation of nanomaterials is high-cost, time-consuming and complex.

Conventional yet promising organic chromophore via in-situ synthesis is an alternative for nanoprobes. As a crucial precursor, o-phenlenediamine (OPD) can be used to synthesize various luminescent substances [20,21]. For example, OPD can combine with the oxidation product of AA (DHAA) to form 3-(dihydroxyethyl)furo[3,4-b] quinoxaline-1-one (DFQ), presenting a strong blue fluorescence emission [22]. Both Ag+ and Cu2+ can oxidize OPD into 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) with a strong orange-yellow fluorescence [23–25]. The DFQ and DAP can assemble into a ratiometric fluorescence probe. Interestingly, not only Ag+ but also Cu2+ can oxidize AA to generate DHAA. Therefore, when the mixture of Ag+/Cu2+, AA and OPD incubates over time, a ratiometric fluorescence system comes into being. Based on these facts, ratiometric fluorescence detection of AA has been realized [26]. Nevertheless, Ag+ is easy to hydrolysis, which limits its further application. The catalytic oxidation of OPD by Cu2+ needs water bath with higher temperature to improve the reaction rate. Compared with Cu2+, Ce4+ has stronger oxidation ability [27]. Moreover, Ce4+ is not easy to hydrolysis. Thus, Ce4+ is a good alternative as oxidizing agent as well as oxidase-mimics [28,29].

Alendronate sodium (ALDS), as a third-generation bisphosphonate, is used to treat bone diseases like osteoporosis and hypercalcemia [30,31]. Nevertheless, it has been reported that excessive ALDS are toxic to soft tissues [32]. Therefore, it is urgent to design a sensitive method to detect ALDS. Fluorescence probes have attracted wide interest for analytical sensing because of their high sensitivity. Dolatabadi and coworkers reported a fluorescence method to detect ALDS through the derivation reaction between ALDS and o-phthalaldehyde (OPA) [33]. Zhang’s group designed a “turn on” strategy to detect ALDS with Cu2+-mediated DNA-scaffolded silver nanoclusters [34]. These strategies are single-signal output through “turn-on” detection mode, which might cause false-positive as well as false-negative interference. Our group developed a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for ALDS with graphene quantum dots [29]. The usage of nanoprobes makes the whole procedure complex and consuming. However, it is still of great significance to fabricate novel ratiometric fluorescence probes for sensing ALDS.

Herein, we designed a novel ratiometirc fluorescence assay for ALDS based on homogeneous chemical reaction. As shown in Scheme 1 , Ce4+ oxidizes AA to generate DHAA, which condenses with OPD to form DFQ with the maximum emission peak at 434 nm. As oxidase-mimics, Ce4+ can trigger the oxidation of OPD into its oxidation product DAP with the maximum emission peak at 568 nm. Thus, a homogeneous fluorescence system with dual-emission is obtained. Once ALDS is introduced, it reacts with Ce4+, inhibiting the generation of DAP. Accordingly, the ratiometric fluorescence signal (F 434/F 568) is affected. The change of F 434/F 568 can allow ratiometric fluorescence detection of ALDS. The Ce4+-triggered in situ fluorogenic reaction holds great potential in sensing application owing to the facile operation and easy availability of raw materials. Moreover, the determination of ALDS in human serum and pharmaceutical samples has been realized with this sensitive and simple ratiometric fluorescence method.

Alendronate sodium trihydrate (ALDS) was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd. OPD, CeS2O8, AA, serine (Ser), alanine (Ala), glycine (Gly), lysine (Lys), histidine (His), glutamic acid (Glu), arginine (Arg), aspartic acid (Asp), valine (Val), methionine (Met), proline (Pro), dopamine (DA), cysteine (Cys), homocysteine (Hcys), glutathione (GSH), uric acid (UA), urea, starch, glucose, lactose, dextrin, and magnesium stearate were provided by Aladdin Company (Shanghai, China). Alendronate sodium tablets were bought from Shijiazhuang Pharmaceutical Group Ouyi Pharmaceutical Co. Ltd. (Batches 152180601).

Fluorescence emission spectra were performed on F-7100 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) spectra were performed on a Bruker AVANCEIII spectrometer (500 M) with tetramethylsilane as an internal standard. The UV–vis spectra were obtained by UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Shimazu, Japan). Electrospray ionization mass spectrometry (ESI-MS) spectra was acquired on Agilent 6545 UHPLC-ESI-Q-TOF mass spectrometer (USA).

Typically, 25 μL ALDS with different concentrations was added into 20 μL Ce4+ (2.0 mM). After reaction for 5 min at room temperature, 400 μL NaAc-HAc buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.0) containing 45 μL OPD (20 mM) and 10 μL AA (0.25 mM) were added to the above mixture. After incubation for 50 min at room temperature, the fluorescence emission spectra were measured under the excitation wavelength at 375 nm.

For analysis of ALDS in human serum, acetonitrile was added into the human serum samples to eliminate protein interference. After centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 15 min, the supernate was diluted 100 times and then analyzed by this ratiometric method.

One alendronate sodium tablet was ground into powder. A certain amount of powder was weighed and then dissolved with 100 mL distilled water. After filtration, a stock solution with 100 μM ALDS was obtained. Finally, the solution was detected with the proposed method.

This sensing strategy involves three processes: (1) Ce4+ oxidizes AA into DHAA, which can combine with OPD to generate DFQ; (2) As oxidase-mimics, Ce4+ can oxidize OPD into DAP directly; (3) ALDS reacts with Ce4+, thus inhibiting the generation of DAP. Firstly, to confirm the formation of DFQ and DAP, the reaction mixtures were separated, purified, and analyzed with 1H NMR and ESI-MS spectra. For DFQ, the number of hydrogen atoms and corresponding theoretical shifts are consistent with the integration of several peaks shown by 1H NMR (Fig. S1). As presented in Fig. 1 A, the ESI-MS spectrum shows a molecular ion signal at 269.0518 (m/z, [M + Na]+), which is in good accordance with the molecular weight of DFQ. For DAP, the ESI-MS spectrum shows a molecular ion signal at 211.0970 (m/z, [M + H]+), which is assigned to DAP (Fig. 1B). In addition, the 1H NMR shows that the peak integrals are consistent with the total numbers of the hydrogen atoms in DAP (Fig. S2). The generation of DFQ and DAP in this Ce4+-triggered reaction were confirmed by the NMR and ESI-MS data.

Fluorescence and absorption spectra were investigated to assess the feasibility of this ratiometric system to detect ALDS. There is no obvious fluorescence emission (curve a, Fig. 2 A) and absorption peak (curve a, Fig. 2B) for only OPD. When Ce4+ is added, a strong fluorescence emission peak at 568 nm as well as a characteristic absorption peak at 418 nm emerge (curve b, Fig. 2), which are ascribed to DAP. Once AA is introduced, the fluorescence intensity at 568 nm decreases slightly. Meanwhile, a new peak corresponding to DFQ occurs at 434 nm (curve c, Fig. 2A). Similarly, the absorbance at 418 nm decreases followed by the generation of a new peak at 348 nm (curve c, Fig. 2B). When OPD and AA is added into the mixture of ALDS and Ce4+, both the fluorescence intensity at 568 nm and the absorbance at 418 nm decrease remarkably (curve d, Fig. 2). However, when ALDS is added into the reaction mixture of Ce4+, OPD and AA, the fluorescence intensities at both 568 nm and 434 nm change slightly (Fig. S3A). It has been reported that ALDS can be oxidized by Ce4+ [35]. We deduce that ALDS prevents the oxidation of OPD from Ce4+ followed by the reducing amount of DAP. To further prove this hypothesis, the effects of ALDS on the fluorescent intensities of Ce4+-OPD system were studied (Fig. S3B). The fluorescence spectra of Ce4+-OPD system change slightly after the addition of ALDS, indicating that ALDS does not react with DAP (curve a,b). Nevertheless, when Ce4+ premixes with ALDS and then OPD is added, the fluorescence intensity ascribed to DAP decreases remarkably (curve c, Fig. S3B). All the results confirm that ALDS prevents the oxidation of OPD from Ce4+, thus changing the fluorescence intensity ratio F 434/F 568. Based on these facts, a ratiomteric fluorescence strategy for analysis ALDS has been developed.

Prior to analysis, some experimental conditions including reaction time, the pH and concentration of each reactant were optimized for Ce4+-OPD-AA system. Fig. 3 A shows the effect of incubation time of Ce4+, OPD and AA on the fluorescent intensities at 434 nm (F 434) and 568 nm (F 568), respectively. As the incubation time increases, F 434 changes slightly while F 568 increases firstly and then reaches to balance when the incubation time exceeds more than 80 min. This indicates that the amount of generating DAP increases gradually with the increasing time and reaches maximum at 80 min. Thus, the incubation time of Ce4+, OPD and AA is set 80 min.

It has been reported that the oxidase ability of Ce4+ is highly dependent on the pH [27,29]. In view of the instability of AA in alkaline conditions, the influences of pH in the range of 4.0–7.5 on F 434 and F 568 were investigated with the results shown in Fig. 3B. With the increasing pH value, F 434 changes slightly while F 568 increases remarkably firstly and then remains constant in the pH range of 5.5–7.5. The results indicate that Ce4+ shows strong oxidase-like activity in the pH range of 5.5–7.5. In view of subsequent practical application, the buffer with pH 7.0 is chosen for the following experiments.

As initiator of ratiometric signal, Ce4+ plays important roles in the whole reaction system. As shown in Fig. 3C, F434 changes slightly while F 568 increases remarkably with the increment of Ce4+ concentrations. Moreover, the F 434/F 568 decreases obviously until it reaches to balance when the concentration of Ce4+ is more than 80 μM. Although Ce4+ influences the amount of formed DAP, excess Ce4+ is not facile for the detection of ALDS. Therefore, moderate 80 μM Ce4+ is chosen in the detection system.

Similarly, the influences from OPD and AA concentration for the sensing system were also investigated. As shown in Fig. 3D, as the concentration of OPD increases, F 434 decreases gradually while F 568 increases remarkably firstly and then reaches to maximum when the concentration of OPD is 1.8 mM. So, we select 1.8 mM OPD as the optimal dosage in the detection system. As shown in Fig. 3E, F434 increases gradually with the increasing concentration of AA. F 568 remains constant when the AA concentration is less than 5 μM and then decreases obviously when the AA concentration is more than 5 μM. Thus, 5 μM AA is selected to obtain strong fluorescence signals.

Finally, the reaction time between ALDS and Ce4+ was investigated with the results presented in Fig. 3F. Both F 434 and F 568 change slightly with the increment of time, indicating that the reaction is very fast. In view of complete reaction, the reaction time between ALDS and Ce4+ is selected to be 5 min.

The sensitivity of this assay for ALDS was investigated with the results shown in Fig. 4 . The fluorescence emission spectra of Ce4+-AA-OPD system were recorded after the addition of varied concentrations of ALDS (Fig. 4A). With the increment of ALDS concentrations from 0 to 70 μM, the fluorescence intensities at 568 nm decrease remarkably while those at 434 nm change slightly. The F 434/F 568 presents a linear relationship against ALDS concentration ranging from 0.1 μM to 10 μM and 10 μM − 70 μM, respectively (Fig. 4B). The regression equations are F 434/F 568 = 0.457 + 0.0609C (R2 = 0.998) and F 434/F 568 = 0.979 + 0.00933C (R2 = 0.990), respectively. The detection limit is as low as 30 nM (S/N = 3), which is much better than those of many other optical methods (Table 1 ).

High selectivity is another crucial factor for construction of sensors. To verify the feasibility of this ratiometric strategy in practical application, some potential interferences were also tested under the same conditions. Fig. 5 A shows the responses of Ce4+-AA-OPD system towards common amino acids (Ser, Ala, Gly, Lys, His, Glu, Arg, Asp, Val, Met, and Pro) and reductive biological molecules (DA, AA, Cys, Hcys, GSH and UA). Fig. 5B shows the effects of metal ions (K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Cr3+), anions (CO3 2−, SO4 2−, Cl−), urea, starch, glucose, lactose, dextrin, and magnesium stearate on the detection of ALDS. The results indicate that no obvious changes are found after the addition of the potential interferences without oxidizability or reducibility. Reductive biological molecules (DA, AA, Cys, Hcys, GSH, UA) and oxidizing metal ions (Cu2+, Fe3+, Cr3+) have remarkable effects on the detection of ALDS. Only when their concentrations are less than 1 μM, their influences on the detection of ALDS can be ignored. Overall, these results indicate that this method has an acceptable selectivity to ALDS, thus it may not be suitable for the direct analysis of complex samples containing high-concentration reductive or oxidizing substances.

To demonstrate practical application of the ratiometric assay, ALDS in human serum samples were detected. Fig. S4A shows the analytical results of this method in the presence of various concentrations of ALDS in human serum samples. As the concentrations of ALDS increase from 0 to 70 μM, the fluorescence intensities at 568 nm decrease remarkably while those at 434 nm change slightly. The F 434/F 568 presents a linear relationship against ALDS concentration ranging from 0.2 μM to 10 μM and 10 μM − 70 μM, respectively (Fig. S4B). A similar linear relationship is obtained in comparison with that obtained in buffer, indicating that the application feasibility of this method in biological samples.

In addition, pharmaceutical sample containing ALDS was analyzed with this method. The analysis result for ALDS tablet with this method (6.2 μM) is in good agreement with real content (6.5 μM). A series of ALDS standard solutions were added into the tablet solutions followed by the fluorescence detection with this method. The satisfactory recoveries of 95.0, 93.0, 106.3% were obtained when 2.0, 10.0, and 30.0 μM ALDS were added (Table 2 ). The above results validate the practical applicability of the ratiometric sensor for ALDS determination in pharmaceutical samples.

In conclusion, a ratiometric fluorescence method has been proposed for highly selective and sensitive ALDS detection with Ce4+-triggered cascade chromogenic reaction. Ce4+ oxidizes AA to generate DHAA, which then condenses with OPD to generate fluorescent DFQ. Additionally, Ce4+ can oxidize OPD to generate fluorescent DAP. The introduction of ALDS inhibits the oxidation of OPD by Ce4+ remarkably. With F 434/F 568 as readout, the ratiometric detection of ALDS can be realized. This ratiometric sensing assay offers a convenient and effective “mix-then-test” way for detection. All the reagents used are commercial and easily available. Thus, it is quite simple and cost-effective in preparation and operation. The whole procedure can be completed within tens of minutes. Moreover, this method is used to detect ALDS in both human serum and pharmaceutical samples.

Meng Xia: Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Fengjin Shi: Validation. Yinghui Xia: Software. Jing Sun: Data curation. Xian-En Zhao: Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Shuyun Zhu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is kindly supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2020MB066).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.119437.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
